segments.s237-g225: گریزان is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'fleeing' used predicatively; بشد is the archaic/poetic past-form of شدن meaning 'became/it happened' here used as 'he took to (that) state' → 'he fled'. پیش افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of Afrasiab' placed after the verb (classical word order flexibility).
segments.s237-g226: This is a parallel predicative construction 'دلش پر ز خون و رخش پر ز آب' = 'his heart full of blood and his steed full of water'. دلش is دل + enclitic -ش 'his heart'. پر ز + N is a poetic/Gathic pattern meaning 'full of N' (پر = 'full (of)'). The coordinating و links two parallel noun‑phrases (possession + 'full of' phrase).
segments.s237-g227: بخفت is a classical past form meaning 'he lay down / he slept / he fell (down)'. از بر is an archaic/preclassical double prepositional sequence meaning 'upon/on (the surface of)'. خاک تیره دراز is a noun with postpositive adjectives 'dark, long' (typical Persian adjective order follows the noun): 'the long dark earth/ground'.
segments.s237-g228: زمانی here = 'after a while/for a time' introducing the temporal frame. بشد is again the classical past 'it became/it happened' used impersonally with هوش ('awareness') to mean 'consciousness returned'. زان functions as a classical connective meaning 'thereupon/therefrom' (hence 'then'/'thereupon'). رزمساز is the agent 'the warrior' (epithet).
segments.s237-g229: چو here = temporal 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The clause follows classical SOV flexibility: 'تهمتن چو پولاد را زنده دید' = 'Tahmten, when he saw Pulād (پولاد را) alive (زنده), he saw/realized...' را is the definite object marker attached to پولاد and زنده دید is the verbal complex 'saw alive'.
segments.s237-g230: This is a verbless noun phrase completed by the final verb دید: 'همه دشت لشکر پراگنده دید' = 'he saw (that) across the whole plain the army was scattered.' همه دشت = 'the whole plain'; لشکر پراگنده = 'the army, scattered'; دید closes the clause as the reporting verb.
segments.s237-g231: دلش تنگ‌تر گشت = 'his heart became tighter/more constricted' (تنگ‌تر is comparative of تنگ, گشت is classical past 'became'). The clause then continues with و linking an action: لشکر براند, where لشکر is the object 'the army' placed before the verb (classical SOV), and براند (from راندن) means 'drove away/put to flight'.
segments.s237-g232: جهاندیده is an epithet 'worldly‑wise' placed before the proper name; گودرز را is the object marked by را; پیش here means 'before (him)' or 'to his presence'; خواند is the past of خواندن used in the sense 'to call/summon' (classical Persian allows adverbial/prepositional elements between object and verb).
segments.s237-g257: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (classical variant of از) and begins a coordinated list of places joined by و 'and'. The sequence names four locales (دریا, دشت, هامون, کوه) in the standard Persian order noun–و–noun, so the whole segment functions as a multi-part adverbial of origin.
segments.s237-g258: سپاه is the subject 'host/army'. اندر آمد is a two-word verb phrase meaning 'came in/arrived' (اندر here marks 'into/in' in older Persian, followed by آمد 'came'). همه همگروه is a postposed descriptive phrase: همه 'all/everyone' modifies همگروه 'fellow-members/comrades', indicating the host arrived with everyone together.
segments.s237-g259: چو opens a temporal clause here meaning 'when'. مردم نماند is 'no people remained' (نماند is past, 'remained/was left' used here in the negative sense). آزمودیم is first-person plural past 'we tested/tried' (verb carries the subject), and دیو is the direct object 'the demon' — the clause thus means 'when no people were left, we tested the demon.'
segments.s237-g260: چنین 'such/so' qualifies the following list. جنگ و پیکار are near-synonymous nouns 'war/battle' and 'struggle/fight' presented in coordinate form, and چندین غریو means 'many/several cries/shouts' — together they specify the manner of testing (i.e. 'in such battles and struggles and many shouts').
segments.s237-g261: سپه را uses the object/topic marker را to place emphasis on 'the army'. چنین صف کشیده is a participial/adjectival phrase 'thus drawn up in ranks' (صف 'battle-line/formation' + کشیده past participle 'drawn/up'), and بمان is an imperative/command meaning 'let it remain / keep (it) so' — together: 'Let the army remain thus, drawn up in ranks.'
segments.s237-g262: تو is the vocative/imperative subject 'you'. با + ویژگان is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the distinguished (men)' (ویژگان here = the notable/chosen). سوی دریا gives the direction 'toward the sea', and بران is an archaic/directional/imperative motion word used here to mean 'go/advance/lead (thither)' — the whole is an order: 'You, with the distinguished, move toward the sea.'
segments.s237-g209: وزان پس is an adverbial phrase ('from that' + 'then') placed before the verb to set the temporal point. بیازید is an archaic/epic past form meaning 'it gaped/opened' (the prefix بی- here is an intensive/poetic element); چون شیر چنگ is a simile ('like a lion (with) a claw'), where چون introduces the comparison and چنگ (claw) follows شیر as the image being invoked.
segments.s237-g210: گرفت is the simple past 'seized/took'. آن is a demonstrative object 'that'. بر here is functioning in the noun phrase that follows (part of the object phrase) rather than as a separate prepositional marker; و simply coordinates two objects ('and'). یال جنگی نهنگ is a compound noun phrase 'the warlike mane of the nahang/whale' where جنگی is an adjective 'warlike' modifying یال and نهنگ names the creature.
segments.s237-g211: بگردن (ب + گردن) is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'upon the neck / onto his neck' placed before the verb. برآورد is here a past transitive verb 'he lifted/put (it) upon' (classical/epic usage of برآوردن). و زد بر زمین coordinates a second action: زد is the simple past 'struck' and بر زمین 'upon the ground' is the prepositional object.
segments.s237-g212: همی is an archaic/progressive adverb meaning 'continually/still' placed before the verb. خواند is past tense 'he called/sang/cried out' (here active, meaning he uttered praise). بر marks the indirect object 'to/toward' and کردگار is 'the Creator/God'; افرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation'—together خواند بر کردگار افرین = 'he cried out praise to the Creator.'
segments.s237-g213: خروشی is a verbal noun meaning 'a roar' or 'a shout'. بر آمد is a common poetic inversion of آمد بر (i.e., 'arose/rose up'). ز ایران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the Iranian army/host' with ز marking origin.
segments.s237-g214: تبیره here is an obscure/archaic modifier preceding زنان; context shows زنان is the subject 'the women' and برگرفتند is the past plural 'they took up / took (to)'; راه is the object 'the road' (idiomatically 'they took to the road' = they fled). (تبیره is not clear in the given senses and likely functions as an adverbial/participial descriptor meaning something like 'in alarm' or 'in confusion'.)
segments.s237-g215: بابر is a proper name (subject). اندر آمد is the poetic word order for 'entered/entered in' (اندر = in/into, آمد = came). دم کرنای is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the blast (دم) of the trumpet (کرنا)' with the genitive relation expressed in classical word order (no ezāfe explicitly written).
segments.s237-g216: خروشیدن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to roar' used here impersonally to indicate that a loud sound rose up. نای و صنج و درای is a simple coordinate list of instruments ('the ney (reed flute) and the cymbal and درای'); the last item (درای) is unclear in the provided sense-list but functions as another instrument in the list, producing the collective 'they roared/sounded'.
segments.s237-g169: چو introduces a conditional ('if') followed by باشی, the present/subjunctive 2nd‑person form 'you be/are'; together چو ... باشی = 'if you are ...'. پیمان‌شکن is a compound adjective/noun 'oath‑breaker' describing the subject 'you'. تیره modifies مغز and together تیره مغز is a poetic adjective phrase meaning 'dark‑minded' or 'of gloomy/ill disposition'. Word order is conditioned for poetry (particle + predicate): 'If you are an oath‑breaker and dark‑minded...'.
segments.s237-g170: نیاید is a negative present (or jussive) form meaning 'does not/will not come' or 'will not appear'. ز دست تو is a genitive/instrumental phrase 'from/at your hand' (i.e. by your doing). پیگار نغز is a noun + adjective 'a fine/skillful battle/effort' (nغز qualifying پیگار). Together the clause means 'no fine exploit in battle will come from your hand.'
segments.s237-g171: تو 'you' is the vocative/subject. این آب روشن is a nominal phrase 'this clear/bright water' where آب can be literal or a metaphor for purity/reputation; روشن = 'clear/evident'. مگردان is a negative imperative 'do not turn/transform' (مـ- negative prefix + گردان 'turn'). سیاه is the resultative adjective 'black'. The clause literally: 'Do not turn this clear water black.'
segments.s237-g172: که here introduces the reason ('for/because') and links to the following verb. عیب آورد is a light‑verb construction 'bring/produce a fault' (آورد used in the sense 'bring about/cause'). بر تو = 'upon you' (showing who will be afflicted), and بر عیب‌خواه = 'to/for the fault‑finder' (بر used here with the sense 'toward/to'). The whole clause: 'for it will bring a fault upon you to the fault‑finder.'
segments.s237-g173: This is poetic word order: بدشنام 'of bad repute / slanderous' modifies زبان 'tongue/speech'. بگشاد is an archaic past form 'opened' (بـ + گشاد), so the clause reads 'Khosrow opened (or let loose) a slanderous tongue' — i.e. he spoke ill. خسرو is the subject/name placed before زبان for emphasis.
segments.s237-g174: برآشفت is a past/inchoative verb 'became angry/was upset'. و = 'and' links the two verbs. شد is the copular 'became', and با + noun here marks the target/object of the suspicion: 'با پسر بدگمان' means 'suspicious toward/of the son' — idiomatically 'he became suspicious of his son.' Note the concise poetic ordering: verb + با + noun = 'became with/against (the) son suspicious.'
segments.s237-g175: بدو 'to him' is an indirect object pronoun meaning 'to/unto him'. گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as the speech verb: 'he said to him'). اگر begins a conditional 'if'. دیو پولادوند is a nominal phrase 'the steel‑clad (or steel‑hearted) demon' — پولادوند is an epithet modifying دیو. The clause is the protasis of a conditional reported speech.
segments.s237-g176: ازین is vowel‑split: از + این 'from/of this' (ازین written as از + ین). مرد بدخواه 'this ill‑wishing man' is the source of harm. یابد here is a present/subjunctive form of یاب 'to find' used in the conditional protasis ('should find' / 'find'), and گزند means 'harm'. Together: 'if (the demon) finds harm from this ill‑wishing man.'
segments.s237-g233: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'; تا introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that they should'); تیرباران is a noun used here as the action 'a shower of arrows' and کنند is the plural subjunctive/optative form 'they do / let them do' placed at the end of the clause (typical Persian verb‑final order). Together: 'He ordered that they should shower (with) arrows.'
segments.s237-g234: هوا را is the definite object ('the air' + object marker را); چو is a simile 'like/as'; ابر بهاران is a nominal phrase 'spring clouds' (ابر = cloud, بهاران = of spring); کند/کنند (here کنند) repeats the verb at clause end meaning 'they make' — so the clause reads 'they make the air like spring clouds.'
segments.s237-g235: ز here is the poetic preposition 'by/from'; ز یک دست + name is a terse idiom meaning 'by one hand (i.e. one strike) [of] Bijan' — دست is literal 'hand' and functions metonymically for 'act/strike'. The repeated pattern 'ز یک دست ... ز یک دست ...' lists agents/assailants.
segments.s237-g236: جهانجوی is an epithet 'world‑seeking' modifying the following proper name رهام (Roham); و simply joins items; گرگین is the proper name Gorgin, followed by نیو which here functions as a short epithet/appendage forming a compound name — the phrase lists two more warriors without an explicit verb (typical nominative listing in epic poetry).
segments.s237-g237: تو گفتی که is 'you said that' (تو = you, گفتی = you said); که introduces a reported/clausal complement; آتش برافروختند is a past plural clause ('they kindled/they lit a fire') with the verb برافروختند at the end following the object/goal (آتش).
segments.s237-g238: جهان را is the definite object marker construction ('the world' + را); بخنجر is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'with a dagger' (بـ + خنجر); همی is an archaic/progressive particle here giving continuous/emphatic aspect ('kept/continually'); سوختند is past plural 'they burned/they suffered burning' — literally 'they kept burning the world with a dagger.'
segments.s237-g239: بلشکر is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'to/with the army/the host' placed before the verb; چنین 'thus' signals direct speech or manner; گفت is the past verb 'said/spoke'; پولادوند is the speaker's name/epithet (literally 'steel‑clad'/'steel‑hearted'), here the nominative subject following the verb — epic word order often places adverbial/agent phrases before the verb.
segments.s237-g240: که introduces the reported clause that follows; the three coordinated noun/adjective phrases 'بی‌تخت و بی‌گنج و نام بلند' lack an overt copula — a normal Persian predicative string meaning 'without throne and without treasure and (with) a lofty name.' Each adjective phrase is juxtaposed and understood as asserting a property of the referent.
segments.s237-g17: کمربند بگرفت is a past action: the noun (کمربند, “belt”) comes before the verb بگرفت (past 'he seized/took'), so literally 'he seized the belt.' The following او را ز زین begins the next clause but continues the same action: او را is the definite object with the object marker را, and ز before زین is the poetic preposition 'from' (زین = 'saddle'), preparing the verbal complement 'lifted from the saddle' in the next segment.
segments.s237-g18: برآورد here functions as a past verb meaning '(he) lifted/removed' (classical Persian usage), taking the preceding object او را ز زین as its patient. The conjunction و links to آسان بزد, where آسان functions adverbially 'gently/lightly' and بزد is the past 'he struck'; بر + زمین is the prepositional phrase 'onto/upon the ground.' The object (او) remains understood across the clauses even though the pronoun is not repeated.
segments.s237-g19: The clause begins with the prepositional phrase به پیگار 'to/toward the battle' (به here = 'toward'), then the pronoun او (he) and the proper name گیو (Giv) as the subject; چون before the verb بنگرید marks the temporal 'when/as.' بنگرید is the past/subjunctive-looking form meaning 'looked' or 'turned his gaze.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'toward the battle he, Giv, when looked.'
segments.s237-g20: This noun cluster is the object(s) of دید ('saw') at the clause end. سر طوس is a genitive-like construction 'the head of Tūs' (the ezāfe is often omitted in this epic style), نوذر is the proper name Nowzar presented in apposition or as another seen person, and نگونسار is an adjectival predicate 'woeful/grief-stricken.' The final دید is the past verb 'saw,' so the whole phrase means '[he] saw Tous's head and Nowzar (who was) grief-stricken.'
segments.s237-g21: برانگیخت is a past verb 'he rose/sprung up.' از جای شبدیز is a prepositional phrase 'from the place of Shabdiz' (شبدیز is the horse's name); in epic diction the object marker را may follow the whole phrase to mark definiteness, so this cluster means 'he sprang up from Shabdiz's place.'
segments.s237-g22: تن و جان ('body and soul') are the entities being adorned; بیاراست is the past transitive 'he adorned/bedecked' (here causative/transitive sense), and آویز را (with را marking the definite object) indicates the ornament used. The idiomatic reading is 'he adorned his body and soul with the pendant' (object marker on آویز signals that the pendant is the thing applied).
segments.s237-g23: برآویخت is a past verb 'he sprang up/leapt upon.' با + دیو here expresses direction/target ('at/against the demon'). چون شیر نر is a simile introduced by چون 'like/as' and modifies the verb: 'like a male lion.' The clause therefore reads 'he sprang at the demon like a male lion.'
segments.s237-g24: This is a nominal apposition/description of the attacker: زره‌دار 'armored (one)' followed by با گرزه 'with a mace' (با indicating the weapon), and گاوسر is a proper name in apposition (the name of the armored man). The phrase qualifies the subject as 'the armored Gāwsar, with a mace.'
segments.s237-g33: دو گرد: numeral + noun (دو + گرد) — in epic Persian گرد here is an archaic noun meaning a 'division' or 'band' (not one of the literal senses listed). از دلیران is a partitive/genitive phrase 'of the valiant' placed after the head noun. پر مایه is an adjectival phrase 'full (پر) of resources (مایه)' modifying دلیران. را is the definite/object marker that makes the whole phrase a definite object; the verb that governs it is supplied later (poetic suspensive order).
segments.s237-g34: This is a stacked adjectival/nominal phrase joined by و: سرافراز 'honored/triumphant' and گرد (again the archaic 'division/band') and گرانمایه 'precious/esteemed'. The final را again marks the phrase as definite/object — repetition like this is a poetic listing: several descriptions of the same or another division are given before the verb appears in the next line.
segments.s237-g35: بخاک اندر افگند is a compact past action: بخاک (to the ground) + اندر (an older preposition 'into/within', here part of the direction phrase) + افگند (past of افکندن 'to throw/cast') = 'he threw (them) to the ground'. و بسپرد uses بسپرد (past of سپردن 'to hand/consign') with the prefixed b-; here it means 'and consigned' and is followed by خوار, an adjective 'despised/worthless', functioning as the result-state ('consigned (them) to humiliation').
segments.s237-g36: نظاره بران دشت چندان سوار: نظاره 'the watching/view' or 'those who look' plus بران ('on that' / 'thereon' — an archaic deictic) and دشت 'plain' give 'on that plain'. چندان سوار literally 'so many riders' — چندان as a quantifier 'many' here modifies سوار 'horsemen'. Word order is poetic: the locative phrase 'نظاره بران دشت' ('watching on that plain') is followed by the quantified noun 'چندان سوار' ('so many horsemen').
segments.s237-g37: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd sing.). بر اختر کاویان is a prepositional phrase: بر 'upon/against' + اختر 'star' used as an honorific title + کاویان 'of the Kayanian' (a possessive/epithet: 'the star of the Kayanian'). The phrase names the person who is attacked; classical Persian often links nouns this way without an overt ezafe between اختر and کاویان in older style.
segments.s237-g38: بخنجر 'with a dagger' (instrumental prepositional phrase). بدو is the short pronominal form 'to him' (direct/indirect object clitic meaning 'him'). نیم کردش میان is a tight phrase: نیم 'half' (adverbially 'in two'), کردش = کرد + ش (past 'did' + enclitic object 'him'), and میان 'in the middle' — together meaning 'he made him into two halves' i.e. 'he cleft him in two.'
segments.s237-g39: خروشی برآمد is a nominal + past-verb 'a cry/roar arose'. ز ایران سپاه is a reversed order (poetic): 'from Iran' (ز ایران) + سپاه 'the host/army' — together 'a cry arose from the Iranian army/host.' برآمد is a common past/perfective of 'to arise/issue forth.'
segments.s237-g40: نماند ایچ گرد اندر آوردگاه: نماند (past negative 'there remained / was not left') + ایچ (classical/poetic 'anything/anything at all' — here 'not even one') + گرد (here again the archaic 'division/band') + اندر آوردگاه 'in the battlefield' (اندر 'in/within' + آوردگاه 'battlefield'). The whole line means 'not a single band remained on the battlefield.'
segments.s237-g9: تهمتن is a proper epithet (Tahmātan) as the subject. بپوشید is a past-tense 3rd-person form (prefix b- + پوشید) meaning “he put on / he donned.” The noun phrase ببر بیان is the direct object: ببر “tiger” functions as a modifier to بیان to name the garment (a tiger-skin cloak); the compound is a poetic noun phrase following the verb (S–V–O word order).
segments.s237-g10: نشست is past 3rd-person “he sat / took a seat.” The small prepositional combination از بر here forms an idiom meaning roughly “upon / astride” (classical phrasing with از + بر). ژنده is an adjective “ragged/worn” modifying پیل “elephant”; ژیان is a poetic element attached to the noun phrase. Together the phrase says he sat on/astride the ragged elephant.
segments.s237-g11: برآشفت is a past 3rd-person verb meaning “he became angry / was enraged.” The conjunction و links the next clause. بر میمنه is a prepositional phrase “on the right (flank)” (بر = on/upon, میمنه = right side). حمله برد is a classical collocation: حمله “attack” + برد used as a light verb meaning “launched / made” (here the verb is the action of carrying out the attack).
segments.s237-g12: ز ترکان is a prepositional phrase using poetic ز (= از) meaning “among / from the Turks.” بیفگند is a past form meaning “he scattered / cast” (here transitive: he scattered them). بسیار modifies the quantity (“many”), and گرد is an adverbial/covering element meaning “about / around”; together they say he scattered many of the Turks in all directions.
segments.s237-g13: ازان پس is a temporal phrase “after that / thereupon” (ازان = thereupon, پس = after). غمی گشت is a predicative phrase literally “he became a grief” i.e. “he grew troubled / sorrowful” (گشت = became). پولادوند is the personal name (Pulād‑vand) which here follows the predicate — Persian allows this flexible ordering.
segments.s237-g14: ز فتراک is “from (his) saddle‑cloth” (ز = from, فتراک = saddle‑cloth). بگشاد is a past form meaning “he opened / drew forth” (here ‘drew out’). پیچان کمند is the object: پیچان “coiled, twisting” modifies کمند “lasso/snare.” The whole clause yields “he drew from the saddle‑cloth a coiled lasso.”
segments.s237-g15: برآویخت is a past 3rd-person verb “he leaped up / sprang up.” با طوس marks accompaniment “with Tūs” (طوس = the hero Tūs). چون پیل مست is a simile “like a drunken elephant” (چون = like, پیل = elephant, مست = drunk/intoxicated used figuratively), describing the manner of his rising.
segments.s237-g16: کمندی is the noun “a lasso” (here presented as an accompanying item). The two following small prepositional phrases specify equipment/placement: به بازوی = “on (his) arm” and گرزی به دست = “a mace in (his) hand.” The repetition of به marks position/instrument, so the line lists his weapons: a lasso on the arm and a club/mace in the hand.
segments.s237-g193: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (root meaning 'keeping') + کن (do!); together it commands 'keep' or 'observe'. به پیمان افراسیاب is the prepositional object: به + پیمان ('to/with the pact') followed by the proper name افراسیاب (the possessor). The whole segment thus means 'observe/keep the pact of Afrasiab.'
segments.s237-g194: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when' (or 'if'). جای + بلا is an attributive noun phrase ('place of calamity' — جای governs بلا as its qualifier). دید is the verbal element 'see/perceive' (here generic/episodic: 'when you/one sees'). و links the two parallel nouns 'جای بلا' and 'جای شتاب' — the latter 'a place/occasion for haste' (جای + شتاب). Together: 'when you see a place of calamity and a place of haste.'
segments.s237-g195: بیآمد is a past/negative compound 'did not come' (here used concessively: 'even if he did not come'). همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle 'still/ever'. دل is the grammatical subject 'the heart'. بیافروزدش is present/future-stative with the old verbal prefix بی- (bi- + افروزد) plus the object clitic -ش 'him': literally 'will/set aflame him' i.e. 'his heart kindles him' (figurative: his heart urges him on).
segments.s237-g196: بکشتی here functions as a locative/means phrase 'by ship / if (he) comes by ship' (poetic compression of به کشتی). درون is an adverb 'inside/within'. خنجر is the instrument 'dagger'. آموزدش (آموزد + -ش) is a third-person present/future form 'teaches him' (used idiomatically: 'the dagger will teach him' = 'he will be learned/struck by the dagger', i.e. stabbed). The sequence expresses a conditional alternative: if he comes by ship, the dagger will deal with him internally.
segments.s237-g197: بدو ('to him') is the dative topical pronoun placed before the verb; گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (classic word order: indirect object + verb + subject: 'to him said Rostam'). که introduces the reported clause. جنگی منم is a nominal predicate: جنگی ('warrior/warlike') + منم (emphatic 'I am'), i.e. 'I am a warrior.'
segments.s237-g198: This segment lists actions/qualities without an explicit conjunction: بکشتی (here used as a deverbal/poetic form related to 'killing/slaying') + گرفتن ('to take/seize') are activities presented together, followed by درنگی منم. درنگی is the noun 'hesitation/delay' used idiomatically with منم to indicate the speaker's stance toward delay: 'I am not one who hesitates' (classical Persian often compresses the negative sense into context). Overall the clause means 'in slaying and seizing I do not tarry.'
segments.s237-g199: شما را places 'you' as the dative/topic ('to you'); چرا is the interrogative 'why' fronted for emphasis. بیم آید is a stative/impersonal construction 'fear comes/afflicts (you)'; همی is the emphatic/continuous particle 'ever/still', giving a rhetorical tone: 'Why should fear ever come to you?'
segments.s237-g200: Again چرا opens a rhetorical question. دل is the subject 'the heart'; به دو نیم is a prepositional phrase 'into two halves' (i.e. 'to split in two'); آید is 'comes/turns' used idiomatically for 'becomes' or 'is split'; همی adds the continuous/emphatic flavor. The clause asks 'Why should the heart be split in two?'
segments.s237-g129: 'تهمتن' is the proper name/epithet (Tahmētan) and is the subject. 'بران' is an adverbial/locative particle here meaning something like 'upon him' or 'thereon', and 'بد' is an adverb ('badly/ill') modifying the action; together they convey 'struck him hard' or 'so severely upon him'. 'که' introduces a result clause; 'مغز سر ش' = 'the brain of his head' with the enclitic 'ش' = 'his' attached to 'سر'.
segments.s237-g130: 'ببیند' here is a result/present-form verb used after 'که' to present the consequence — basically 'was seen' / 'became visible'. 'پر از' is the adjectival phrase 'full of', with 'پر' = 'full (of)' governing the following 'از رنگ تیره' ('of a dark color'). The final 'برش' in this poetic line functions as a locative/relational element ('on it' / 'upon its surface') in context (an older/poetic form); the clause as a whole states that the exposed brain was covered with a dark color.
segments.s237-g131: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'پولادوند' is the name/epithet (Pūlādvand). 'از بر زین' is a small prepositional phrase meaning 'from/on the saddle' (literally 'off the saddle'), and 'بماند' is the past/remote form of ماندن ('remained/was left') used idiomatically to mean he fell/was left off the saddle.
segments.s237-g132: 'تهمتن' is the subject again. 'جهان‌آفرین' is the object (the 'World‑Creator') and is marked by the object marker 'را'. 'بخواند' is the past/remote third person form of خواندن here meaning 'called/summoned' (i.e., he invoked or cried out to the Creator).
segments.s237-g133: 'که‌ای' is an archaic/poetic relative/vocative construction ('who are' / 'O you who'), addressing the addressee. 'برتر از' = 'higher/better than', and 'گردش روزگار' is a genitive phrase 'the turning/revolution of fate/time' (i.e., the cycles or turns of fortune).
segments.s237-g134: A string of epithets in apposition: 'جهاندار' (sovereign/ruler), 'و' = 'and', 'بینا' (sighted/discerning — here 'all‑seeing' as a divine attribute), 'و' = 'and', 'پروردگار' (Lord/Nurturer). The nouns are coordinate and describe the addressee invoked in the previous clause.
segments.s237-g135: 'گرین' is an elided poetic form = 'گر این' ('if this'); it introduces a conditional. 'گردش جنگ' = 'the turn/course of war' and 'من داد نیست' = 'there is no justice for me' ('داد' = justice; 'نیست' = does not exist). The clause expresses the condition 'if in this course of battle I get no justice'.
segments.s237-g136: 'روانم' = 'my soul' (possessive -م). The sequence 'ب دان' here functions as 'بدان' = 'to/for that, knows/considers' (ب = prepositional/prefix, 'دان' = 'to know' / 'to regard'), linking to the following clause 'گیتی آباد نیست' ('the world is not prosperous'). So the whole second clause says that the speaker's soul judges/knows that the world is not well‑ordered/prosperous.
segments.s237-g105: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The verb بشنید is past tense 'heard' with پولادوند as its subject; این سخن is the direct object ('these/the present words'), following the common Persian word order Subject–Object after the past verb in archaic/poetic phrasing.
segments.s237-g106: بیاد آمدش is a light idiom 'came to his memory' (بیاد = 'into memory', آمد + enclitic -ش = 'came to him'); گفته‌های کهن is a nominative/accusative phrase 'the old sayings' (گفته‌های = plural 'sayings/statements', کهن = 'ancient'). The two parts together mean the ancient sayings occurred to him mentally upon hearing the words.
segments.s237-g107: که begins a proverbial/relative clause ('for' / 'that'); هر کو is an archaic 'whoever' construction (هر = 'every/any', کو = relative 'who' in older Persian). ببیداد is an adverbial/phrasing 'by injustice/with cruelty', جوید is a general-present verb 'seeks' used proverbially, and نبرد is the object 'battle/struggle' (i.e. 'seeks battle').
segments.s237-g108: This clause states the result: جگر خسته باز آید is literal 'the heart (جگر, figurative seat of feeling) comes back weary' (جگر + adjective خسته; باز آید = 'returns/comes back'), and و روی زرد adds 'and his face is pale' (روی = face, زرد = pale). The verbs are in the present/gnomic form common in poetry to state a general consequence.
segments.s237-g109: گر at the start is the conditional 'if'. از دشمنت literally 'from your enemy'. بد رسد = 'bad/evil comes' (بد = bad, رسد = 'arrives/ befalleth'); the second گر + ز دوست repeats the conditional for the alternative 'or if it comes from a friend'. The line uses parallel conditionals to introduce the proverbical advice that follows.
segments.s237-g110: بد و نیک را marks 'the bad and the good' as definite objects (را). داد دادن here is a verbal-nominal construction: داد (justice/payment/due) + دادن (to give) = 'to give what is due / to administer justice'. نکوست is the predicate 'is good/fitting'. Together: 'to give each his due, whether bad or good, is right.'
segments.s237-g111: همان is a demonstrative 'that very' or 'the same'; رستمست is an archaic fused copula (رستم + -ست, old form of است) meaning 'is Rostam' or 'is like Rostam' depending on context; این‌که introduces the following relative clause 'who / the fact that …'; مازندران names the place (Mazandaran) and is the locative/frame for the relative clause.
segments.s237-g112: شب تیره is the noun + adjective 'the dark night'. بستد is past tense 'he shut/closed' (archaic 3rd‑singular). بگرز گران is a compact poetic phrase: grammatically it can be read as instrumental ('به گرزِ گران' = 'with a heavy mace') or as a contracted verb‑plus‑adverb; the sense in context is instrumental — 'with a heavy/weighty mace' (گران = 'heavy/weighty'). The line thus says he 'shut/overthrew the dark night with his heavy mace.'
segments.s237-g41: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing three subjects: the proper names 'فریبرز' and 'گودرز' joined by the conjunction 'و' (and), followed by 'گردنکشان' (a plural substantive/epithet). The conjunctions link the items in the usual left-to-right order; 'گردنکشان' is an epithet-like plural noun (literally 'neck‑cutters') functioning as part of the subject group.
segments.s237-g42: 'گرفتند' is the simple past (3rd person plural) meaning 'they seized/took' and connects back to the subject list. The prepositional particle 'از' marks source ('from'), 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that', 'دیو جنگی' is a nominal phrase 'the war‑demon' where 'جنگی' is a postpositive adjective 'warlike/of war', and 'نشان' is the direct object 'token/trophy'. The word order is somewhat inverted (verb before source phrase and object), a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s237-g43: 'بگفتند' is past tense (3rd person plural) 'they said'/'they told' — the prefixed 'ب' is an archaic or poetic verbal prefix found in older Persian. 'با' here functions idiomatically as the oblique marker with a person (roughly 'to' or 'with' Rostam), 'رستم' is the proper name, and 'کینه‌خواه' follows in apposition as an epithet meaning 'the avenger' or 'the vengeful one'.
segments.s237-g44: The complementizer/relative 'که' introduces reported content ('that'). 'پولادوند' is an adjectival epithet used substantively ('the steel‑clad one' or 'steel‑hearted'), and 'اندرین رزمگاه' is a locative phrase: 'اندرین' = 'in this' (classical locative) + 'رزمگاه' = 'battlefield'. The clause thus states the presence/location of that steel‑clad fighter in this battlefield; the copula is omitted as usual in Persian.
segments.s237-g45: 'بزین' here is an archaic compound (compare به زین) meaning 'to the saddle' or 'to mount' (adverbial notion 'to ride/up on the saddle'). 'بر' is the preposition 'on/upon', 'یکی' is the indefinite 'one/any', 'نامداری' functions as a substantive 'a renowned/notable (man)', and 'نماند' is the past 3rd person singular 'there remained not / none remained'. Together the phrase means 'there remained not one famed man to mount (i.e. there was not a single famous man left to ride)'.
segments.s237-g46: The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') means 'from'. 'گردان لشکر' is a genitival/compound expression 'the troop/division of the army' (گردان modifying لشکر), 'سواری' means 'riders/horsemen', and 'نماند' repeats 'there remained none'. The clause states that no mounted men were left from the army's ranks.
segments.s237-g47: 'که' again introduces a subordinate clause ('so that'/'that'). 'نفگند' is an archaic verbal form (here negative past/passive) meaning roughly 'was not thrown/cast'; the verb root 'فگند' corresponds to classical 'انداختن' (to throw) with the negative prefix 'ن-' producing 'did not throw / was not thrown'. 'بر خاک' is the prepositional phrase 'onto the ground', and 'پولادوند' is the patient/subject 'the steel‑clad (man)'. The order is verb + locative + subject, a poetic inversion.
segments.s237-g48: This is a list of instruments introduced by the bound prepositional prefix 'ب-' meaning 'with/by' attached to the nouns (e.g. 'بگرز' = 'with a mace', 'بخنجر' = 'with a dagger', 'بتیر' = 'by/with an arrow'), joined by 'و' (and); the final item 'کمند' (lasso) appears without the prefix but is part of the same list. The phrase names the weapons that nonetheless did not cast him to the ground.
segments.s237-g201: گر introduces a conditional ('if'). نیستتان is the present negative 'is not / you do not have' with the plural/2nd-person suffix -تان, so the clause says 'if (to) you is not'. جنگ را places 'battle/war' as the domain/topic (را as the def. object/topic marker). زور و دست are coordinated nouns meaning 'strength and power/hand' — together: 'if you lack strength and power in war'.
segments.s237-g202: دل من is the topical/subject phrase 'my heart'. بخیره is an adverbial/phrase meaning roughly 'for good / entirely' (classical spelling of به‌خیر). نباید is the impersonal/modal 'should not / must not', and شکست (here used as a verbal idea) means 'be broken/defeated'. Together: 'my heart must not be broken for good.'
segments.s237-g203: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle here functioning like 'even/indeed' or an intensifier attached to the conditional. این جادوی بی‌خرد is the noun phrase 'this foolish magic/sorcery' (جادوی = magic/sorcery; بی‌خرد = 'unwise, senseless').
segments.s237-g204: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / in respect of' used here with پیمان یزدان 'the covenant of God'. همی + بگذرد is a classical present/subjunctive/future-like construction ('would pass / transgress / pass beyond'); بگذرد is 'to pass' or 'to go beyond' in this context, meaning 'to transgress/leave the covenant'.
segments.s237-g205: شما را marks 'you' as the topical/affected party (را as topic/emphasis). ز پیمان شکستن is a gerundial/nominal phrase 'about/because of breaking the covenant' (ز = 'concerning / from'), and چه باک is an idiomatic interrogative 'what fear' = 'why fear / what need to fear'.
segments.s237-g206: گر again introduces a conditional 'if'. او is 'he'. ریخت here denotes the action 'poured / cast' (i.e., he casts). بر تارک خویش خاک is a fixed image 'dust upon his own brow/head' (بر = 'on/upon', تارک = 'top/brow' in the idiom, خویش = 'his own' formed from خوی + the clitic ش), meaning he humiliates or covers himself with dust — so 'if he throws dust on his own head.'
segments.s237-g207: من آکنون is the subject with temporal adverb 'I now'. سر دیو پولادوند is a noun phrase 'the head of the steel-clad / iron-armed demon' (پولادوند is an epithet 'steel-clad' modifying the demon). The nominal sequence is elliptical of the classical genitive ('سرِ دیوِ پولادْوند').
segments.s237-g208: بخاک اندر آرم is a verb phrase meaning 'I put / I cast into the earth/to the ground' (بخاک = 'to the ground', اندر = 'into/in', آرم = 'I place / I lay / I cast' in a classical form). ز چرخ بلند adds the source or vantage 'from the lofty sky/heavens' (ز = 'from', چرخ = 'sky/heaven', بلند = 'lofty/high'), so the whole says he casts the demon's head down from the high heavens to the ground.
segments.s237-g57: This clause is an adverbial/locative phrase followed by a passive verb. اندرون 'inside/within' gives the location, پیش من 'before/in the presence of me' marks that the killing happened in front of the speaker, and کشته شد is a passive past ('was killed') with شد functioning as the auxiliary. برزم is an archaic/poetic element placed up front to set the scene (see senses).
segments.s237-g58: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' modifying the following nominal phrase. اختر و روزِ من is the possessed subject ('my star and my day' — a poetic way to mean fortune/day), and گشته شد is a periphrastic result/state: گشته (a participle, 'become/turned') plus the auxiliary شد ('has been / has become'), producing 'has been turned' or 'is made so'.
segments.s237-g59: A coordinated noun phrase: 'جوانان و من' (the youths and I) is the coordinated subject; the adjective زنده follows and qualifies them ('alive/still living'). با + noun phrase (با پیر سر) is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with the old man at (our) head' — سر here is 'head' in the sense 'leader' or 'at the head'. Word order is typical for Persian: subject phrase + postpositive adjective + accompanying prepositional phrase.
segments.s237-g60: This is an optative/exclamatory construction: 'مرا شرم باد' = 'shame be upon me' (باد is the optative particle 'may/let it be'). Here the object pronoun + object-marking را are written as separate tokens (م + را). از introduces the cause/reason ('because of' / 'on account of') and kَلَاه و کمر are the items causing shame ('hat and belt').
segments.s237-g61: Two coordinated short clauses using prefixed-perfect/preterite forms: کمر برگشاد ('the girdle/girding was opened/loosened') and کله برگرفت ('heads were raised' / 'the head was lifted up'). برگشاد and برگرفت are compound past forms with the prefix بر- giving the sense 'up/away' or an inceptive/resultative nuance common in epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s237-g62: Another coordination: خروشیدن ('to cry out, to roar') and ناله اندر گرفت ('they took up moaning/they began to lament'). Here اندر introduces the internal object/scene ('into/among'), and گرفت is a simple past 'took/seized' used idiomatically with ناله to mean 'took up lamentation' or 'began to moan'.
segments.s237-g63: چو + past verb (بشنید) introduces a temporal subordinate ('when he heard'). رستم is the subject. دژم گشت is a small predicate: دژم (grim/sullen) is a predicative adjective and گشت ('became') is the copular/turning verb, while سخت functions adverbially to intensify ('very/strongly'), so 'he became very grim/ stern.'
segments.s237-g64: بلرزید is the simple past 'he/it trembled' (intransitive). برسان here is used with برگ درخت as its object: taken together the clause describes a violent trembling that affected the tree's leaves — 'he/it trembled so that the leaves of the tree were shaken' (برسان used in the sense 'bring/affect' or 'cause to move'). برگ درخت is a genitival phrase 'leaf of the tree.'
segments.s237-g89: تبه گشته is a compound participial/passive phrase meaning 'been ruined/turned bad' (perfect participle) that modifies the following noun. اسپ بزرگان is a genitive construction 'the steeds of the great (nobles)', with the adjective/participle preceding the noun (typical poetic order). بتیر is an agent/ablative phrase 'by (an) arrow(s)' placed after the noun phrase to indicate cause.
segments.s237-g90: بدین سان is an adverbial idiom 'in this manner' (here split into elements; treat as one unit). برآویخته is a past participle of برآویختن meaning 'reared up' or 'raised up' describing the horses' posture. خیره is an adjectival/participial description 'staring, astonished' that goes with برآویخته. خیر here functions as a short exclamatory/poetic particle (archaic) expressing wonder or lament—its precise sense is idiomatic.
segments.s237-g91: بدو is the prepositional/indirect-object phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت here functions as the past-tense reporting verb 'said' (the provided lemma list gives a related nominal sense; syntactically it is the verb). پولادوند‌ای is a vocative/epithet ('Polādvand' with a vocative -ای) and دلیر is an appositive epithet 'brave' describing that person.
segments.s237-g92: جهاندیده، نامبردار و شیر are a series of epithets joined by و ('and') that further describe the speaker (Polādvand). The final شیر is metaphorical 'lion' indicating bravery; the whole string functions as apposition or a vocative description attached to the name.
segments.s237-g93: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that') reporting what was said. بگریزد is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'would flee' or 'flees' in a general/hypothetical sense; the verb appears before its adverbial phrase (از پیش تو). ژنده پیل is a noun phrase 'ragged elephant' with ژنده (an adjective/descriptor) preceding پیل.
segments.s237-g94: ببینی is a present/subjunctive/optative form 'you see' / 'may you see' used here as an exhortation or vivid remark. کنون ('now') is an adverb modifying the following NP, موج دریای نیل is a genitive chain 'the wave (surge) of the sea of the Nile' (i.e. the Nile's surge). The verb-front order gives immediacy: 'now you see the Nile's wave.'
segments.s237-g95: نگه کن is an imperative idiom 'look! behold!' with نگه (gaze/watch) plus the imperative کن. کنون ('now') is an adverb of time, and آتش جنگ من is a genitive phrase 'the fire of my war' (آتش modifies جنگ; من marks possession). The verb phrase plus NP functions as an imperative attention-getter.
segments.s237-g96: A coordinated list of nouns joined by و ('and')—کمند و دل و زور و آهنگ—each element is governed by the final possessive من ('my'), so the whole list means 'my snare, my heart, my strength, and my intent/aim.' This is a typical Persian construction where the single possessive after a series applies to every item in the series.
segments.s237-g217: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). پولادوندست is the name/epithet plus the copula (poetically written together) meaning 'Polâdvand is/was' or 'the steel‑man'; بیجان شده is a perfective participial phrase 'has become lifeless/was rendered dead' (شده = 'became/has been made'). Together this clause states the thought: 'that Polâdvand had become lifeless.'
segments.s237-g218: بران = بر آن 'on that' (locative unit) + خاک 'the earth/ground'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', مار پیچان شده = 'had become twisted/was contorted like a snake' (پیچان = 'twisting/writhing', شده = 'had become/was'). The whole segment describes the corpse's posture on the ground.
segments.s237-g219: گمان برد is a past‑tense phrasing meaning 'he supposed/he formed the thought'; رستم is the thinker (subject). که introduces the subordinate clause describing what Rostam supposed: 'that Polâdvand ...'. Note that برد here is part of the verb phrase 'گمان برد' rather than its literal senses listed below.
segments.s237-g220: ندارد is the negative present/presumptive 'does not have' used in the subordinate clause ('he thought that ... did not have'). بتن = 'on the body' (locative phrase). در درست here functions as 'a proper/in-place' (در + درست: 'in proper [condition]'), ایچ is the emphatic 'nothing/none', and بند is 'fetter/fastening/strap' — so the phrase says literally 'has not on the body any proper fastening' i.e. 'has no proper armor/fastening at all.'
segments.s237-g221: برخش is the horse's name (poetic form) and دلیر is an epithet 'the brave'. اندر آورد is a past tense compound 'brought in/put in' (آورد = 'brought/caused'); پای is the object 'foot/hoof'. Word order places the named horse and epithet before the verb and then the object 'پای'. The sense here is that the horse drove its hoof in.
segments.s237-g222: بماند (past/resultive) 'remained/was left'. آن تن اژدها = 'that body of the dragon' with را marking the definite object/topic. بجای = 'in place'/'there' indicating that the dragon's body stayed where it was. Together: 'the dragon's body remained in its place.'
segments.s237-g223: چو = 'when'. پیش صف = 'before the line/ranks' (prepositional phrase). آمد is simple past 'came'. یل شیرگیر is the subject 'the hero, the lion‑catcher' (an epithet following or attached to the noun). The phrase sets the time/place: 'when the lion‑catcher heroic figure came to the front of the ranks.'
segments.s237-g224: نگه کرد = 'he looked/kept watch/paused' (کرد used as auxiliary to form the verbal phrase). پولاد is the agent here (a person named Polâd), برسان is the verb 'to deliver/send/let reach' used here as 'sent/launched', and تیر is the object 'arrow'. The clause means 'Polâd aimed/caused the arrow to be sent.'
segments.s237-g73: بیفشارد is a past/actional verbal form meaning 'struck/pressed' with the object (ران) placed immediately after it; ران رخش را is a noun phrase where ران (the horse's flank/haunch) is the object and رخش را uses را as the definite object marker after the possessor; تیز کرد uses the adjective تیز 'swift' followed by کرد 'made/caused' to express 'made (it) swift' (causative construction).
segments.s237-g74: برآشفت is a past form meaning 'it grew agitated / became startled' (perfective state); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; آهنگ here means 'direction/intent/pace' (i.e., the motion of charging) and آویز کرد is a light-verb construction in which آویز (a root meaning 'to pounce/hang' in this poetic context) combines with کرد 'made/did' to mean 'made to pounce / set off to charge.'
segments.s237-g75: بدو گفت is a common Persian sequence where بدو is a prepositional/dative pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb گفت 'said' (past). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; دیو ناسازگار is the noun 'demon' followed by the adjective ناسازگار 'discordant/uncooperative' postpositively modifying it.
segments.s237-g76: ببینی is the present/subjunctive (used here with future force) 'you shall see' (second person form used as a prophetic/future assertion); کنون is the adverb 'now'; گردش روزگار is a genitive/compound where گردش 'turn/circuit' is qualified by روزگار 'time/fate' to mean 'the turn of fate'. Word order is typical Persian: verb (ببینی) near start, then temporal adverb and the object phrase.
segments.s237-g77: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; آواز رستم is a genitive phrase 'Rostam's voice/cry' with the possessor after the noun; بگردان here is a verbal/lexical element derived from بگرد (imperative 'turn') used idiomatically in the line (poetic usage) and رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived.' Together they form 'when Rostam's cry reached (them/that place).'
segments.s237-g78: تهمتن (an epithet for Rostam) is the subject/name; یلان را is the definite object marker را attached to یلان 'heroes/warriors'; پیاده is an adverb/adjective 'on foot / dismounted'; بدید is the past-tense verb 'saw.' Word order places the object phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s237-g79: دژم گشته is a perfect/resultant phrase: دژم 'grim/sullen' with گشته (perfect participle of شدن) 'has become' indicates a change of state 'became grim.' زو is the poetic contracted form ز 'from/because of him' (here 'around him' / 'on his account'); چار گرد is a compound reading as 'a fourfold circle / a band of four' (چار 'four' + گرد 'round/about') and دلیر postpositively modifies گرد as 'brave.' The sequence describes a four‑man brave circle forming around him.
segments.s237-g80: چو is the comparative/conjunction 'like' here; گوران و دشمن are two nouns joined by و 'and' (in Ferdowsi's diction گوران can be a tribal/name word or a poetic reference in context); بکردار is a compound adjective formed with the prefix ب‑ plus کردار 'deed/behaviour' meaning 'of such conduct / behaving thus' and modifies شیر 'lion' used metaphorically. The whole simile compares those gathered to lions in their bearing toward the enemy.
segments.s237-g137: روا دارم is an idiomatic present-tense phrase: روا (an adjective 'fitting/permissible') with دارم ('I have') yields 'I have the right / it is fitting for me'. از دست here is a prepositional phrase 'by the hand of / at the hands of' that names the agent (پولادوند) responsible; پولادوند is a proper-name/epithet ('steel-clad').
segments.s237-g138: روان مرا برگشاید is the clause giving what is 'fitting': روان 'soul/spirit' with مرا (the enclitic object pronoun 'me' split here into م + را; را is the definite-object marker) marks 'my soul' as the object. برگشاید is a third-person jussive/optative (poetic) meaning 'may/should free/open', and ز بند is a prepositional phrase 'from the bond/fetter'.
segments.s237-g139: ور is the conditional 'if'. افراسیابست is a fused classical-form with the copula (i.e. 'is Afrasiab'), and بیدادگر is the predicate noun 'tyrant/unjust one'. The whole segment = 'If Afrasiab is a tyrant.'
segments.s237-g140: This line is elliptical. تو مستان = 'you are drunken/intoxicated' (مستان used predicatively). ز من دست و زور و هنر is a prepositional phrase 'from me (there is) hand, strength, and skill' with the copular/verb omitted; the sense is that 'I have no hand/power, strength, or skill' (i.e. those resources are gone from me).
segments.s237-g141: که‌گر is a conditional 'if'. من شوم کشته is a classical passive/hypothetical construction: شوم (subjunctive 'I should become') + کشته (past participle 'killed') = 'if I should be killed'. بر دست اوی means 'by his hand' (بر + دست + اوی where اوی is the possessive/3rd-person reference).
segments.s237-g142: بایران نماند یکی جنگجوی states the consequence: بایران (a locative reference to the land/realm — written here in a fused form) + نماند 'will not remain' + یکی جنگجوی 'a single warrior'. The negation is carried by the sense of نماند after the locative, so the phrase means 'not one warrior will remain in the land.'
segments.s237-g143: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative pair 'neither ... nor ...'. مرد کشاورز 'a farmer/man who tills' and پیشه‌ور 'artisan/professional' are listed as things that will not remain or exist (continuing the previous negative sense).
segments.s237-g144: A continuation of the negative listing: نه خاک و نه کشور نه بوم و نه بر — 'neither soil nor country, neither homeland (بوم) nor land/region (بر)'. These pairs are simply nominal items enumerated under the same negative scope as the previous line.
segments.s237-g97: کزین پس: an adverbial phrase 'from this (time) on / after this' (کز + این/پس in archaic poetic ordering). نیابی is the negative 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive of یاب (here functioning as a future/imperative-like prediction): 'you will not find.' ز شاهت نشان is the object phrase 'a sign/trace of your king' where ز = 'of/from' and the possessive -ت on شاه marks 'your'. Word order is verb before its object (poetic VSO inversion).
segments.s237-g98: نه ... از نامداران و گردنکشان: نه is the negation ('not'); از here means 'among / from (the class of)'. نامداران = 'the renowned/illustrious ones' and گردنکشان = 'the proud/arrogant ones' (literally 'neck‑stretchers'); the phrase restricts the domain in which no trace will be found.
segments.s237-g99: نبینی is the negative 2nd‑person singular of دیدن ('you will not see' / 'you shall not behold'). زمین is the direct object 'the earth / the land' (here 'the world / wakeful life'). زین سپس is an adverbial 'from this / henceforth' (an archaic poetic 'ز این' construction followed by سپس 'afterwards'). جز بخواب = 'except in sleep' (جز = 'except', بخواب = b- + خواب 'in sleep' or 'while sleeping').
segments.s237-g100: سپارم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of سپردن used as 'I will entrust / I shall give over.' سپاهت = 'your army' (سپاه + possessive -ت). بافراسیاب is the prepositional phrase با + فراسیاب meaning 'to/with Afrasiab' (i.e. 'I leave/entrust your army to Afrasiab'). The clause is a single verbal clause with verb first (poetic order) followed by object and beneficiary.
segments.s237-g101: چنین گفت رستم بپولادوند is the reporting clause 'thus said Rostam to Puladvand.' چنین = 'thus / so.' گفت is the verb 'said' (here the provided sense list lacks the verb sense, so treat as the past‑tense verb). رستم is the speaker's name. بپولادوند is 'to Puladvand' (ب- prefix 'to' + پولادوند, an epithet/name meaning 'steel‑clad' used as the addressee).
segments.s237-g102: که تا چند ازین بیم و نیرنگ و بند begins an indirect question introduced by که + تا چند = 'how long / until when'. ازین (از + این) = 'from this / by this' pointing to the present condition. بیم = 'fear', نیرنگ = 'deceit / trickery', بند = 'bond / restraint / fetter.' The phrase asks how long he will remain under these conditions.
segments.s237-g103: ز جنگ آوران تیز گویا مباد: ز = 'from / among'; جنگ آوران = 'war‑bringers' = 'warriors' (a compound meaning those who bring battle); تیز = 'swift/keen' modifying آوران; گویا = 'it seems / as if' introducing the feared possibility; مباد is an optative negative 'may it not be' (wishing that the following possibility not occur). The whole is a wish against the appearing of such a warrior.
segments.s237-g104: چو باشد دهد بی‌گمان سر بباد: چو = 'when / if'; باشد is 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative 'it be' (conditional subclause); دهد is 3rd‑person singular of دادن used causatively 'he will cause / will give (to effect)'; بی‌گمان = 'undoubtedly / surely'; سر = 'head'; بباد is an old passive/jussive form meaning 'be blown away / be lost'. Together: 'if/when that happens, surely the head will be blown away' (i.e. he will behead or cause ruin).
segments.s237-g161: نبینی is a negative predictive/subjunctive form meaning 'you will not / you do not see' (here predicting outcome). ز marks source ('from'), گردان is the collective 'troop/host', ما is the 1st-person plural pronoun 'our/us' placed after the noun (typical Persian word order: noun + possessive). جز introduces the exception 'except', and گریز is a noun 'flight/retreat' — the clause therefore says 'you will see from our ranks nothing except flight.'
segments.s237-g162: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. خیره here is an adjectival/participial state 'astonished' (addressing the addressee). با + چرخ گردان forms an oblique phrase 'with the turning wheel' (چرخ گردان = 'turning/revolving wheel' — an image of fate), and ستیز functions as a verbal noun/infinitival idea 'to strive/fight'. The whole admonishes: 'Do not, astonished, fight the turning wheel.'
segments.s237-g163: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the verb 'said' (past) placed after the adverb as normal; با here introduces or attaches to the epithet and name (an older/poetic use marking accompaniment/agent), so شیده افراسیاب is read together as 'the mad (shideh) Afrasiab' who is the speaker. Word order is X گفت (said X) with the epithet following the particle با.
segments.s237-g164: که introduces a result/completion clause 'so that/that'. شد is the past of 'become' ('became'). مغز من is the possessed noun phrase 'my mind/brain' (noun before the possessive pronoun). زین سخن is a prepositional phrase 'from these words' (z- + in = 'from this/these'); پرشتاب is an adjective 'hurried/agitated'. The clause literally: 'that my mind became hurried from these words.'
segments.s237-g165: برو is the imperative 'go'. تا introduces purpose/result 'so that' and is followed by the subjunctive ببینی 'you may see' (تا + subjunctive = purpose clause). که introduces the content clause 'that/which' and پولادوند is the person being referred to (an epithet/name 'Polādvand' = 'steel-clad'). The sequence is a typical Persian purposive construction: 'Go so that you may see that Polādvand ...'.
segments.s237-g166: بکشتی here functions as a compressed/poetic prepositional phrase 'by/aboard ship' (بِ + کشتی) rather than the verb 'you killed' — context and word order favor a locative/means reading. همی is an archaic/adverbial particle meaning 'still/thus' or marking the continuous/characteristic manner. چون is 'like/thus', کند is the present/factual verb 'does/makes' (3rd person), and دست بند is a compound noun 'hand-fetter/shackle' (دست = 'hand', بند = 'fetter'). Together: 'aboard the ship, thus he fastens a hand-fetter.'
segments.s237-g167: چنین is 'thus'. گفت normally is 'said' (here past) but the provided sense list does not include the simple verb sense (so sense index is unavailable). شیده is the epithet 'the mad/love-struck one' applied to the speaker. که begins the reported content: 'that [the following was true].' پیمان شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's covenant/agreement' (noun before possessor).
segments.s237-g168: The clause begins with the negation (نه) + این بود 'this was not' (the negation is explicit in the line though the provided first token is 'این'); با او is 'with him', بپیش is a prepositional/adverbial form 'to the front/before', and سپاه is 'army/host'. Put together: 'This was not (the king's) covenant — that he should go before the army with him.'
segments.s237-g177: نماند is a negative-perfect/past-form meaning “there remained not / was not left”; it introduces a negative existential statement. بدین رزمگه is a prepositional phrase (‘in/at this battlefield’) where ب- + this (بدین) marks location; زنده کس follows, with زنده (alive) qualifying کس (person/anyone). Together the word order is literal: “remained-not in-this battlefield a living person.”
segments.s237-g178: ترا is the clitic form meaning ‘to you / for you’ (dative), written here as ت + را; از introduces the source/cause (‘from / because of’), هنرها is the plural ‘skills/arts’, and زیانست is a predicative noun/adjective ‘is harmful / is a loss’ (older copular formation). و بس is a fixed concluding phrase ‘and that is enough / and that’s all.’ The sequence literally reads: ‘To you from the skills is harm — and that’s all.’
segments.s237-g179: عنان (the reins) is the direct object of برگرایید, an older/poetic past-form meaning ‘turned (the reins)’ or ‘directed’ (compare برگرداند). و آمد links actions: ‘and he came.’ چو شیر is a simile ‘like a lion’ modifying آمد. Word order is object + verb then conjunction + verb + simile.
segments.s237-g180: به introduces the goal (‘to / into’), آوردگاه is ‘the place of coming’ = ‘battlefield/arena’, and دو مرد دلیر is a genitival phrase ‘of the two brave men’ (دو modifies مرد, دلیر qualifies مرد). The whole phrase is a prepositional phrase of direction: ‘to the battlefield of the two brave men.’
segments.s237-g181: نگه (a noun meaning ‘gaze, watch’) plus کرد forms a verbal phrase ‘he watched’ or ‘he looked on.’ پیکار is ‘battle/fight’; دو پیل مست is a descriptive noun phrase ‘two elephants (giants) intoxicated/maddened’ — here مست is figurative (‘frenzied, raging’). The phrase means literally ‘he watched the battle of two maddened elephants.’
segments.s237-g182: درآورده is a perfect/participle of درآوردن ‘to bring out/put forth’ (here ‘having thrust out / set’), followed by بر یکدگر ‘against one another’; هر دو دست (‘both hands’) completes the idea: word order is slightly inverted for emphasis, literal: ‘having brought (their) both hands upon one another.’ It describes the fighters’ posture — both have put their hands against each other.
segments.s237-g183: بپولاد is a proper/name-epithet (literally ‘steel-clad’ or a name derived from پولاد) and is the speaker. گفت is the past verb ‘he said’ (here rendered as the narrative verb introducing direct speech). ای سرافراز شیر is a vocative address: ای ‘O!’, سرافراز ‘high/exalted’, شیر ‘lion’ (honorific metaphor).
segments.s237-g184: بکشتی‌گر is a vocative noun ‘O wrestler’ (here used as an address or title). آری is an interjection ‘yes / indeed.’ مر is the dative clitic ‘to me’ (older form of مرا/بر من), او را is the object ‘him’ with the object marker را attached, and بزیر (‘to/down’) here functions as a directional/verb-like phrase meaning ‘bring/throw down’ — so the clause is an imperative/request: ‘Yes — bring him down to me.’
segments.s237-g145: کشتی گرفتن is a compound verbal noun + infinitive meaning 'to wrestle' (here expressed as a single action). نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن 'to place / to set' used here with the sense 'they began / they set about'. روی after the verb functions as a local/linking preposition 'upon' or 'facing' and connects this clause to the group described in the next segment.
segments.s237-g146: This is a nominal phrase that names the combatants: دو ('two') + گرد + سرافراز ('proud/triumphant') and دو جنگجوی ('and two warriors'). گرد here marks the rounded group/figures being described (epic usage for a heroic person/group) and سرافراز is an epithet modifying them; the conjunction و links the next noun phrase.
segments.s237-g147: بپیمان is a compact/poetic expression meaning 'by agreement' or 'they made a pact' (the stem پیمان 'pact' with a prefixed form). که introduces the content of that agreement ('that...'). از هر دو روی سپاه literally 'from each of the two sides of the host' — از = 'from', هر = 'each/every', روی here = 'side', سپاه = 'army/host'.
segments.s237-g148: This clause states the prohibition: بیاری 'aid/assistance' (from یار) is the object; نیاید is a negative jussive/imperfect form meaning 'shall not come / should not appear'; کسی 'anyone' (used with the negation to mean 'no one') and کینه‌خواه 'avenger' or 'vengeful one' identifies who must not come. Word order is emphatic: object — verb — subject — modifier.
segments.s237-g149: میان = 'between' and سپه = 'the army/host' mark the location relation. نیم فرسنگ بود = 'there was half a farsang' — نیم 'half', فرسنگ a unit of distance, بود is the past copula 'was' (here impersonal: 'there was'). The phrase describes the distance separating the hosts.
segments.s237-g150: ستاره نظاره بران جنگ بود is a clause with ستاره 'star' as subject, نظاره 'watching/observation' as a nominal predicate or light verb construction meaning 'was watching', بران = 'on/over that' and جنگ 'battle/fight'. بود is again the past copula 'was'. Together: 'a star was watching over that fight.'
segments.s237-g151: چو here is the conjunction 'when'. 'پولادوند و تهمتن' are two named/comparative figures (epithets/names). بهم = 'to/toward one another' (reciprocal). The clause sets the temporal scene for the following action: 'When Puladvand and Tahmatan toward one another...'.
segments.s237-g152: برآویختند is the 3rd-person plural past of the verb برآویختن 'to spring upon / to lash out', meaning 'they leaped / sprang upon'. آن دو شیر is a metaphorical subject 'those two lions' (i.e., fierce men) and دژم is an epithet 'grim/fierce' modifying شیر. The clause reports the violent action that follows the temporal clause.
segments.s237-g249: ز خون جوانی که بد ناگریز is a prepositional phrase headed by ز (poetic 'from/of') marking the source or cause: 'from the blood of the youth'. که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the modifier بد ناگریز; بد here is an intensifying/pejorative adjective ('ill/unfortunate') and ناگریز is an adjective meaning 'inevitable' — together they qualify the youth as one whose doom was lamentably unavoidable.
segments.s237-g250: بخستی دل ما بپیکار تیز is a verbal clause whose main verb is بخستی (second-person past/you-made), here meaning 'you caused/turned'. دل ما is the object 'our heart', and بپیکار تیز is a phrase of result/aim: ب- (preposition + noun) + پیکار 'to battle/fighting', with تیز ('keen/sharp') as an adjective modifying the attitude — 'you have made our heart keen for battle.' Word order is poetic (verb before object), and بخستی links this whole clause back to the cause in the previous segment.
segments.s237-g251: چه باشی که با تو کس اندر نماند is a rhetorical/exclamatory clause. چه starts the exclamation 'what (use/for what purpose)', and باشی is a subjunctive/present-form verb ('be / would be') used idiomatically in 'چه باشی که ...' = 'what good is it that...'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that no one remains with you' (با تو کس اندر نماند: with you no one remains/lingers). The syntax expresses contempt or a dismissive hypothetical.
segments.s237-g252: بشد دیو پولاد و لشکر براند begins with بشد, here used optatively/command-like ('let it be' / 'may it be'), followed by the subject phrase دیو پولاد (the demon Polād — Polād is a name/title derived from 'steel') and the coordinated predicate لشکر براند 'and drive away the host'. براند is subjunctive/optative in tone matching بشد. The line therefore expresses a wish or hypothetical: 'let the demon Polād rise and drive away the army.'
segments.s237-g253: همانا ز ایرانیان صد هزار begins with همانا 'verily/truly' as an emphatic adverb. ز introduces the group 'from the Iranians' (i.e., among the Iranians / belonging to the Iranians), and صد هزار is the numeral phrase 'a hundred thousand' giving the quantity. The clause asserts an abundant number on the Iranian side.
segments.s237-g254: فزونست برگستوان‌ور سوار is a verbless/compound predicate in which فزونست ('is more / there are more') asserts the abundance, followed by the descriptive compound برگستوان‌ور سوار: برگستوان‌ور is an epithet meaning 'mighty / powerful-armed' and سوار 'riders' (horsemen). Word order places the predicate/adjective phrase after the quantity for poetic emphasis: 'a hundred thousand more — mighty, strong-armed horsemen.'
segments.s237-g255: بپیش اندرون رستم شیر گیر is an imperative/commanding sequence. بپیش ('forward / onward') and اندرون ('into the interior/midst') set the direction; رستم is the vocative/name addressed; شیر گیر is an imperative 'take/seize the lion' (گیر as imperative of 'to seize'). The phrase orders Rostam to advance inward and seize the lion, with the direction words placed before the vocative for dramatic, poetic effect.
segments.s237-g256: زمین پر ز خون و هوا پر ز تیر is a pair of verbless descriptive clauses linked by و. زمین پر ز خون = 'the ground is full of blood' and هوا پر ز تیر = 'and the air is full of arrows.' Here پر ز (full of) is a poetic adjectival construction (پر followed by ز + noun) describing how the battlefield is saturated; the two halves mirror each other for parallel, vivid imagery.
segments.s237-g121: بزد is a past‑tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'he struck/hit' placed before its instrument (تیغ) in inverted, poetic word order; و simply links the two actions ('and'). بند کمندش is a noun phrase: بند ('fetter, cord') + کمندش (کمند + ش, the possessive suffix 'his/its' = 'his snare/its lasso'). برید is the past of بریدن 'to cut' and completes the coordinated action ('and [he] cut the snare's cord').
segments.s237-g122: بجای آمد is a small phrase where بجای ('in the place') precedes آمد (past 'came/appeared'), creating 'appeared in its place'; the verb آمد remains the main predicate. آن بند بد را is a definite noun phrase ('that bad/evil bond') marked by را (the definite/direct‑object/topic marker) and placed before کلید ('key'). In this poetic inversion, کلید is the noun that 'came' (subject-like in English), while آن بند بد را indicates the thing to which the key pertains—hence 'the key of/for that evil fetter appeared'.
segments.s237-g123: بپیچید is the past tense 3rd‑person form of پیچیدن 'to turn' (here 'he turned'). زان پس is a temporal adverbial ('from that, after that' = 'then/afterwards') with زان ('from that') + پس ('after'). سوی دست راست is the directional phrase 'toward the right hand' (سوی 'toward' + دست 'hand' + راست 'right'); the object of بپیچید is expressed as a prepositional/locative phrase.
segments.s237-g124: بدانست is past 'he knew' (بد- prefix + دانست), introducing the clause that follows. کان is an archaic/connective particle here (classical 'that'/'it was that') — its exact gloss is archaic and not listed among the provided senses. روز روز is a doubled noun typical of poetry for emphasis ('day after day' or 'that very day'); بلاست is بلا + است ('is calamity/misfortune'), an assertive predication. Overall: 'he knew that that day was calamity.'
segments.s237-g125: عمودی is an adverb/adjective 'vertical(ly)' describing the manner of strike; بزد is past 'he struck'. بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'upon his head' (بر 'on/upon' + سر 'head' + ش 'his'). پیلتن follows as the proper name (here the target/person/place): the whole means 'he struck Pīltan on the head with a vertical blow.' Poetic word order places manner/adverb before verb.
segments.s237-g126: که introduces a result/circumstantial clause ('so that/whereupon'); بشنید is past 'heard' (3rd person). آواز او is a noun phrase 'his voice/cry' (آواز 'voice' + او 'his'); انجمن is the agent/scene 'the assembly' or 'the gathering' that heard it. The clause thus reports the consequence: 'so that the assembly heard his cry.'
segments.s237-g127: چنان تیره شد is a typical resultive construction: چنان ('so, thus') + تیره ('dark') + شد (past 'became') = 'became so dark'. چشم پولادوند is the subject noun phrase 'the eye(s) of Pūlādvand' (چشم 'eye' + پولادوند, here a name/epithet). The adjective and verb precede the subject in this poetic ordering.
segments.s237-g128: که introduces the result clause 'so that/that'. دستش is 'his hand' (دست + ش). عنان را is 'the reins' marked by را (definite object/thing concerned). نبد is an archaic negative/past form meaning 'was not/did not' (here 'could not'/'did not manage'). کار بند is a compound: کار ('work/doing') + بند ('fetter/binding'), i.e. 'the task of binding' or 'the fetter's operation.' Together the clause says his hand could not manage/perform the work of the reins/binding.
segments.s237-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. بنمود is an archaic/past-form verb (3rd singular) 'showed/displayed' and in Persian poetry the verb can precede its subject; here خورشید تابان (the shining sun) follows the verb. درفش is the direct object 'banner/standard' placed at the end of the clause.
segments.s237-g2: معصفر شد is a copular predicate: معصفر (yellowish/saffron-colored) + شد (past of شدن, 'became'). The demonstrative آن ('that') precedes the noun پرنیان (silken cloth), and the adjective بنفش (purple) follows the noun in normal Persian order. The whole phrase is inverted for emphasis: the predicate ('became yellow') appears before the noun phrase it applies to.
segments.s237-g3: تبیره is the noun 'announcement/interpretation/explanation' acting as the subject; برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز + درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's court/threshold' (ز marking source, درگاه شاه = 'the king's presence/court'). The clause follows normal Persian order subject–verb with an origin phrase introduced by ز.
segments.s237-g4: The sequence places a proper name (بابر) at the start (here a personal name or vocative insertion), then the compound verbal phrase اندر آمد ('came in/entered') in past tense. خروش سپاه is a genitive phrase 'the roar/cry of the army' and in poetic word order the noun phrase that the verb concerns can follow the verb; the whole reads as the entrance of Babur and the coming in of the army's cry.
segments.s237-g5: بپیش سپه is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in front of / at the head of the army' (ب- + پیش). بود is the past copula 'was/stood', and پولادوند is the predicate (a descriptive name/adjective 'steel-clad, Polādvand'). In poetry the predicate can follow the copula, producing 'stood Polādvand before the army'.
segments.s237-g6: بتن زورمند is a compound descriptive phrase: بتن ('on the body' / 'bodily') + زورمند ('mighty') — 'mighty in body'. و is the coordinating 'and'. ببازو is a locative/instrumental phrase 'on/at the arm' (ب- + بازو), and کمند is the noun 'lasso/snare' placed after it. Together this continues the description: 'mighty-bodied and with a lasso on his arm.'
segments.s237-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. صف برکشیدند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb phrase: صف ('battle line/formation') + برکشیدند ('they drew up/raised'), where the verb precedes or accompanies the noun in poetic inversion. هر دو سپاه ('each/both armies') is the subject phrase (here 'both armies'); in poetry the subject can follow or be distributed around the verb.
segments.s237-g8: This is a parallel pair of copular clauses using شد ('became') with predicate adjectives: هوا شد بنفش = 'the air became purple' and زمین شد سیاه = 'the earth/ground became black'. The conjunction و links the two balanced clauses; note the adjective follows the copula as is normal in Persian.
segments.s237-g185: بخنجر is an instrumental phrase (ب- + خنجر) = “with/ by a dagger” placed before the rest; جگرگاه او را is the definite object (جگرگاه = the breast/innermost part, او = him, را = object marker). بکاف is a predicative adjective/verb meaning “is enough” or “will suffice” placed after the object (poetic word order). Together the phrase means “a dagger in his breast is enough.”
segments.s237-g186: هنر (skill/ability) is the subject and باید is the modal auxiliary ‘it is necessary/one must’; از کار کردن is an ablative/agentive gerund phrase ‘by/from doing (work, deeds)’ where کردن is the verbal noun/infinitive, and نه لاف is the negative contrast ‘not boasting.’ The clause says that what is required is concrete skill in action, not mere bragging.
segments.s237-g187: نگه کرد is a light verb construction where نگه (gaze/watch) combines with کرد (past of کردن) to mean ‘looked/fixed his gaze.’ Subject follows the verb (گیو = Giv) and اندر افراسیاب is the locative phrase ‘at/onto Afrasiab.’ This is a typical classical Persian verb–subject order.
segments.s237-g188: بدان here functions as an adverbial 'thereupon/at that' (poetic fusion of به + آن); خیره گفتار is a noun phrase ‘astonished/staring utterance’ with خیره modifying گفتار; و چندان شتاب adds ‘and such haste.’ The whole segment is an adverbial phrase modifying the previous action (Giv’s gaze).
segments.s237-g189: برانگیخت is past tense ‘roused’ (transitive) with اسپ (steed) as its object: ‘he roused/spurred his horse.’ The conjunction و links to the next clause. Note the compact verb + object order common in poetry.
segments.s237-g190: برآمد is an intransitive past ‘came forth/rose/set out’; دمان (the skirts/edge) is given without an explicit preposition (a common poetic omission) and indicates the place/source ‘from the skirts’ or margin. چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when’; بشکست (broke) is past passive/inaction of شکستن with پیمان (the covenant) as its object; همی is an adverbial/emphatic particle and بدگمان is the predicate adjective ‘(he) suspicious’ — together: ‘When the covenant was broken, he was/grew suspicious.’
segments.s237-g191: برستم here is a proper-name subject (a character speaking). چنین گفت is a fixed expression ‘thus said’ (چنین = thus, گفت = said/spoke); کای is the vocative particle ‘O’ and جنگجوی is the addressed noun ‘warrior.’ Word order places the speaker’s name before the report ‘thus said,’ followed by the vocative address.
segments.s237-g192: چه is the interrogative ‘what’; فرمان دهی is a verbal phrase where فرمان (command) is the direct object and دهی (2nd‑person present/future of دادن) asks ‘will you give/ do you give’; کهتران را is the marked object/indirect object with را (here ‘the lesser/younger ones’ or subordinates) and بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive ‘say/tell.’ The clause is a direct question and imperative combined: ‘What command will you give? Tell (it) to the lesser ones.’
segments.s237-g65: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' (verb before subject for narrative compression). بنزدیک is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'near/close' that attaches to the verb; پولادوند is the name/epithet placed after it, so the clause reads roughly 'Polādvand came near.'
segments.s237-g66: ورا is the enclitic object/pronoun 'him' (to him); دید is the past verb 'saw' (main verb of this clause). برسان is used poetically as a verbal/adverbial notion of 'reaching/approaching' (from رساندن) and takes کوه بلند ('the high mountain') as its phrase, so the sequence means 'he saw him coming up to/the way of the high mountain.'
segments.s237-g67: سپه را: سپه is the direct object 'the army' marked by را. همه modifies سپه meaning 'the whole/entire (army)'. بیشتر خسته is a comparative/adverbial phrase 'more tired' describing state; دید is the past verb 'saw' taking the whole phrase as its object: 'he saw the entire army more tired.'
segments.s237-g68: وزان is a fused conjunctional particle 'and from that/and therefrom' linking to the previous clause. روی پرخاش is a genitive-like phrase 'a face of reproach' (روی = face, پرخاش = reproach/aggression), پیوسته is an adverb 'constantly', and دید is the past verb 'saw' — together: 'and from their faces he saw continual reproach.'
segments.s237-g69: بدل گفت is an idiom 'in reply he said' (بدل = 'in return/answer', followed by گفت 'said'). کین here functions as an older/dialectal demonstrative 'this' modifying روز ('day'); ما is the possessive 'our'; تیره گشت is a verbal phrase 'became dark/bleak' (تیره = dark/ominous, گشت = became).
segments.s237-g70: سرنامداران ما is the subject phrase 'our leading men/chieftains' (سرنامداران = leaders). خیره گشت is a predicate consisting of خیره 'astonished/stunned' + گشت 'became' (past), so 'our leaders became astonished/stood aghast.'
segments.s237-g71: همانا is an emphatic 'verily' or 'indeed'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that...' or is a connector. برگشت پرگار ما is a metaphorical noun phrase: برگشت 'the turning/backwarding' + پرگار 'compass' (figurative for course/direction) + ما 'our' — 'the reversal of our compass' (i.e., our course has turned).
segments.s237-g72: غنوده شد is a compound verb 'became slumbering/fell asleep' (غنوده = asleep/slumbering, شد = became/was). آن bِخت بیدار ما is a noun phrase 'that wakeful fortune of ours' (آن = that; بخت = fortune/fate; بیدار = awake/alert; ما = our), so the line says our formerly alert fortune has now gone to sleep.
segments.s237-g241: چرا introduces a rhetorical question. سر (object) precedes the verb as is common in Persian poetry. همی is an archaic/emphatic adverb here (emphasizing the action). داد is the past of 'to give' (he gave). باید + بباد is a modal construction: باید 'must/ought' plus بباد (present-subjunctive/impersonal poetic form 'be blown away / be lost'), together meaning 'ought to be given off / be cast to the wind.'
