The whole clause asks why one should give up one's head to the wind.
segments.s237-g242: چرا again marks a question. کرد is past tense of 'to do/make' used here with a causative sense ('caused/made'). باید is the modal 'must/ought', همی is emphatic, and رزم یاد is a noun phrase 'remembrance of battle' (رزم 'battle' + یاد 'remembrance/memory'). The clause asks why the memory of the fight should be (made to be) — i.e., 'why make one remember the battle?'.
segments.s237-g243: سپه را is the definite object (را marks the object) placed before the verb for poetic order. بپیش اندر is an adverbial phrase 'forward / into the front/middle'. افگند is the past 'threw/cast' (classical/poetic equivalent of انداخت). و رفت is the conjunction 'and' plus رفت 'left/went'. Together: 'He cast the army forward and departed.'
segments.s237-g244: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/against'. رستم is the governor of the prepositional phrase (i.e. 'against Rostam' / 'because of Rostam'). همی is emphatic. بند جانش is a genitive compound: بند 'fetter/bond' + جانش 'his life' (جان + enclitic -ش). بکفت is a past tense/formal poetic verb meaning 'sufficed / was enough'. The clause means roughly 'against Rostam, even the fetter of his life sufficed' — i.e. Rostam alone was adequate to decide his life.
segments.s237-g245: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here treated as the speech verb). پیران is the subject 'the elders', and بافراسیاب is a prepositional phrase 'with Afrasiab' (meaning 'to Afrasiab' / 'addressing Afrasiab'). The clause introduces reported speech: 'Thus the elders said to Afrasiab.'
segments.s237-g246: که introduces the reported clause 'that...'. شد is past 'became'. روی گیتی is a noun phrase 'the face/surface of the world' (روی 'face/surface' governing گیتی 'world'). چو is the simile 'like/as', followed by دریای آب 'a sea of water' (دریای, ezāfe omitted in poetry, + آب 'water'). So: 'that the face of the world became like a sea of water.'
segments.s237-g247: نگفتم is the negative past 'I did not say' (classical negation with نگفتن). که introduces the subordinate clause. با رستم 'with Rostam' and شوم is the present-subjunctive first-person 'I may become / I should be' used here in the sense 'I would join/enter into (something)'. دست here is the noun 'hand' used idiomatically ('to clasp hands' = make peace/ally). So the clause means 'I did not say that I would clasp hands with Rostam.'
segments.s237-g248: نشاید is an impersonal predicate 'it is not fitting/it ought not to be'. درین is the fused prepositional phrase 'in this' (در + این), کشور 'country/realm'. ایمن is an adjective 'safe', and نشست is the noun/verb 'sitting/settling' used here as the action 'to sit/settle safely'. Together: 'It is not fitting in this country to sit safely.' — i.e. one cannot remain secure here.
segments.s237-g81: 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the verb; 'گفت' is a past-tense verb 'said' (3rd sg.) forming the clause 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said'. 'با' here marks the addressee ('to/with') and is followed by the address 'کردگار جهان' = 'the Creator of the world.' Note the poetic order (adverb + verb + indirect object).
segments.s237-g82: This is a direct address. The sequence 'که‌ای' functions like the inverted vocative 'ای که' = 'O you who...'; 'برتر از' is the comparative 'higher/superior to' with the preposition 'از' introducing the standard of comparison; 'آشکار و نهان' are the two nouns 'the manifest and the hidden.'
segments.s237-g83: The phrase shows inverted classical word order: 'مرا چشم' = modern 'چشمِ مرا' ('my eye' / 'my sight'); 'اگر' introduces the conditional 'if'; 'تیره گشتی' is a perfective/resultative 'have become dark/dim' (گشتی = became/are made), and 'بجنگ' is an imperative 'fight!' addressed to companions. Overall: 'If my sight becomes dim, (then) fight.' Note that 'مرا' here is the possessive suffix + object marker split across the line (poetic segmentation).
segments.s237-g84: 'بهستی' is an archaic/poetic verbal form related to 'بودن' (here giving an outcome/state: 'you will be / you will become' or 'you will suffer/be affected'); 'ز' = 'from/because of'; 'دیدار' = 'seeing, sight' (here 'the act of seeing'); 'این روز تنگ' = 'this narrow/strained day' — 'تنگ' conveys tight/difficult circumstances. The clause states the consequence: '(you) will be affected by seeing this narrow day.'
segments.s237-g85: 'کزین سان' = 'from this manner / from this (event)'; 'برآمد' is the past tense 'arose, issued forth'; 'ز ایران' indicates the place/people affected ('from/throughout Iran'); 'غریو' is the noun 'a cry/roar' and is the thing that 'arose.' The verb precedes its nominative noun in this poetic order.
segments.s237-g86: This continues the list of sources of the cry. The first 'ز' = 'from'; 'پیران' = 'the elders'; 'هومان' is a proper name; the second particle 'وز' here functions like 'and from / and the blast of' (poetic 'وز' = gust/blast); 'نره دیو' = 'the manly/male demon' (an emphatic epithet). Altogether: 'from the elders and Houman, and from the blast of the manly demon.'
segments.s237-g87: 'پیاده شده' is a compound adjective/participial phrase 'having become on-foot' i.e. 'dismounted' (پیاده = on foot; شده = become/done). It is followed by the list of names 'گیو و رهام و طوس' as the subjects who have dismounted. Poetic order places the participle before the names.
segments.s237-g88: 'چو' = 'as/like' introducing a simile; 'بیژن' is the proper name used as the exemplar; 'که' = relative pronoun 'who/that'; 'بر' = preposition 'upon/on'; 'شیر' = 'lion'; 'کردی' is a past tense form 'did/made' (an archaic/poetic past ending here agreeing with the relative subject); 'فسوس' is a noun meaning 'a hiss/insidious whisper' (the sound Bijan made). So: 'like Bijan who made a hiss at the lion.'
segments.s237-g113: بدو (an older prepositional/clitic form) here means “to him” and comes before the verb; گفت is the reporting verb (“said”) in the past, so the phrase literally reads “to him said.” کای is the vocative particle “O,” addressing someone directly. مرد رزم‌آزما is a nominal phrase with an epithet: مرد (“man/hero”) modified by رزم‌آزما (a compound meaning “tested in battle” or “battle‑prov­ing”), the ezāfe linking is conventional in poetry and often left implicit.
segments.s237-g114: چه باشیم is a deliberative/interrogative construction: چه (“how/what”) + باشیم (first‑person plural subjunctive/optative of بودن) asks “what shall we be/do?” or “how shall we fare?” برخیره is an adverbial/poetic particle giving a purposive or evaluative sense (roughly “for our safety/for advantage”); چندین is a quantifier “many/several,” and بپای is an archaic verb form meaning “to remain/stand (ready)” — together they ask whether they should hold/stand in many places.
segments.s237-g115: بگشتند is the past plural verb “they turned/returned.” وز is an archaic/prepositional variant of از (“from”), placed before its complement; دشت is the noun “plain.” برخاست is a past intransitive (“rose/rose up”), and گرد here is an adverbial/postpositional element meaning “around/about,” so the clause describes things rising up from around the plain.
segments.s237-g116: This is a nominal series describing the combatants: دو پیل (“two elephants”) + ژیان (an epithet; poetically qualifying the elephants) + و (and) + دو شیر (“two lions”). نبرد is used as the action of the line — here a past verbal sense “fought” (poetic word order allows a nominal list followed by the verb).
segments.s237-g117: برانگیخت is a past/telic verb “roused/roused himself” or “arose.” آن باره is a temporal phrase “that moment/occasion” (آن = that, باره = occasion/instance). پولادوند is an epithet meaning “steel‑clad” or “armored/steel‑hearted,” functioning as the subject of the verb.
segments.s237-g118: بینداخت is the past of انداختن (“threw/cast”), here meaning he cast/shot something. پس is an adverb/conjunction “then/after that”; تاب‌داده is a participial compound “whirled/put into a spin” (تاب + دادن) modifying کمند, and کمند is the direct object “lasso/lariat” — together: “then he cast a whirled lariat.”
segments.s237-g119: بدزدید is a past verb “it stole/snatched (away).” یال is the direct object “mane.” آن modifies the following noun phrase (“that”), and نبرده is an adjective/participle phrase meaning “not taken/unseized” (i.e. the rider still mounted). سوار is the head noun “rider/horseman.” The cluster describes that rider whose mane was torn.
segments.s237-g120: چو is a subordinating particle “when/as”; زین (“saddle”) + گونه (“manner/way”) form the adverbial phrase “in this manner/from the saddle” (i.e. in this way of mounting/fighting). پیوسته شد is a two‑word predicate: پیوسته (adjective/adverb “continuous/continuous­ly”) + شد (past of شدن, “became”), and کارزار is the noun “battle/struggle.” Together: “when in this way (from the saddle) the battle became continuous.”
segments.s237-g25: Object-before-subject word order is poetic here: 'کمندی' (the lasso) is placed first, followed by the past verb 'بینداخت' (he threw/cast). 'پولادوند' is the agent (an epithet/name, 'the steel-clad') appearing after the verb. 'بینداخت' is past tense, 3rd-person (he threw).
segments.s237-g26: 'سر گیو' = 'Giv's head' (possessive relationship expressed by simple juxtaposition in poetry rather than an explicit ezafe). 'گرد اندر آمد' is a compact poetic phrasing meaning 'came around/about' (گرد + اندر آمد = 'went/wound in/around'). The final verb 'ببند' expresses the result 'it tightened/closed' — in poetry this short form functions like an imperative/resultative (here: the noose tightens).
segments.s237-g27: The verbal phrase 'نگه کردن' (to watch) appears as the noun 'نگه' plus the light verb 'کرد' (made/did) — together 'نگه کرد' = 'they watched/kept watch'. The subject 'رهام و بیژن' follows the verb (poetic inversion). 'ز راه' is a prepositional phrase 'from the road/way'. Note that classical poetic lines sometimes show number/verb agreement different from colloquial norms.
segments.s237-g28: Here 'بدان' is the fused prepositional phrase 'به + آن' meaning 'to/at that' or 'with that', pointing back to something previously mentioned. It governs the coordinated list 'زور و بالا و آن دستگاه' = 'that strength and loftiness and that formation/arrangement.' The coordinating 'و' links the nouns; 'دستگاه' means a system/formation (military arrangement).
segments.s237-g29: 'برفتند' is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they went/advanced'. The conjunction 'تا' introduces a purpose clause here ('so that / in order that'). 'دست پولادوند' is the object phrase 'the hand(s) of Pouladvand' (possessive relationship expressed by juxtaposition).
segments.s237-g30: 'ببندند' is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person plural used in a purpose clause introduced by 'تا' — 'that they may bind'. 'هر دو' (implicit here as 'هر' + following 'دو') means 'both'; 'بخم' is an archaic/poetic word describing the loops/coils; 'کمند' is the lasso. Together 'هر دو بخم کمند' = 'with both coils/loops of the lasso.'
segments.s237-g31: 'بزد' is past tense 3rd-singular 'he struck'. 'دست پولاد' is the direct object 'Polad's hand' (the name/title follows the possessed noun without an explicit ezafe in this poetic line). 'بسیار هوش' ('very much awareness') is an adverb + noun phrase that functions as the object of the verb in the following clause ('to arouse much awareness'). The action and its effect are split across the verse line-break (enjambment).
segments.s237-g32: 'برانگیخت' is past tense 3rd-singular 'roused/aroused' and takes 'اسپ' (the steed/horse) as patient/affected subject in this context: 'he greatly aroused the horse's awareness.' The conjunction 'و' connects this to 'برآمد خروش' where the verb 'برآمد' (arose/issued forth) precedes the noun 'خروش' (a cry/roar) — another example of poetic inversion.
segments.s237-g49: This is a nominal clause describing state. همه رزمگه = “the whole battlefield” (literal word order: quantifier + place-noun). سربسر is an adverbial intensifier placed after the subject meaning “throughout/entirely.” ماتمست is an older/poetic fused form meaning “is in mourning” (ماتم + -ست, the archaic copula ‘is’), so the phrase says the battlefield is wholly in mourning.
segments.s237-g50: بدین کار is an adverbial/prepositional phrase meaning “because of/for this matter” (بدین = به + این in older usage). فریادرس is a nominative noun “rescuer/succor.” رستمست is a poetic form where رستم is followed by the copula -ست (archaic ‘is’); the clause therefore predicates that the rescuer is Rostam (predicate noun + subject/copying order is typical in poetry).
segments.s237-g51: ازان پس is an adverbial time phrase “after that/thereupon.” خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive “the roaring” or “to roar,” and ناله qualifies it as a lament (“a cry of lamentation”). خاست is the simple past archaic form meaning “arose/ began.” Together the phrase means “then a roar/cry of lamentation arose.”
segments.s237-g52: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” قلب here functions as ‘the heart/center’ (of the host), then the coordinating و links the next phrase. چپ لشکر = “the army’s left” (left wing), and و دست راست = “and the right hand” (right wing/side). Word order is compressed/poetic but means the cry came from the center and both wings of the army.
segments.s237-g53: چو = “when.” کم شد is a poetic/intransitive past construction meaning “became few/was diminished” (i.e., were lost or died). ز گودرز indicates source/possession (“of Goudarz”) and هر دو پسر is the subject phrase “both sons.” The sequence places the verbal idea (کم شد) before the explicit subject for poetic effect: “when both sons of Goudarz were lost.”
segments.s237-g54: بنالید is the past plural of نالیدن “they wailed/lamented.” با here functions in the sense of ‘in the presence of / before / to’ (poetic usage) introducing the addressee. داور دادگر is a noun+adjective/title “the just judge” (داور = judge, دادگر = just/righteous), so the clause says they bewailed (their loss) before the righteous arbiter.
segments.s237-g55: که introduces reported content (“that”). چندین = “several/many.” نبیره پسر is a compound noun meaning “offspring/descendant (sons)” — a plural possession/kin phrase. داشتم is the first-person past “I had.” So this clause reports: “that I had many descendants/sons.”
segments.s237-g56: همی is a habitual or continuous marker in older/poetic Persian here meaning “always/used to.” سر = “head.” ز is the poetic preposition “from/of/than” linking سر to خورشید; idiomatically سر ز خورشید بگذاشتم conveys holding/placing one’s head toward or above the sun (an image of pride or exaltation). بگذاشتم is the first-person past “I placed/ I used to place” (ب- prefix + گذاشتم). Together: “I would always set my head toward/above the sun.”
segments.s237-g153: همی is a propective/continuous adverb meaning 'continually/kept (on)'; it frames the action as iterative. دست is the concrete noun 'hand(s)' and is the direct object of the following verb. سودند is a poetic/archaic verb form agreeing with a plural subject (third person plural); here it conveys the action 'they did/seized/engaged' but the provided dictionary senses do not match this verbal use, so treat it as an archaic past/plural verb meaning 'they seized/laid hold (of)'. با + دگر is a prepositional construction 'with one another' (با = 'with', دگر = 'other/one another'), linking the mutual action.
segments.s237-g154: گرفته is a past participle / perfect-formation meaning 'having taken' or 'seized' and modifies the implicit subject. جنگی here functions adjectivally/nominatively as 'warlike' or 'warrior' (i.e. 'the fighter(s)'). دوال is an old/poetic numeral/adjective 'twofold, both' qualifying کمر ('waist' or 'girdle'), so the phrase describes grabbing the opponent at the waist/belt (both waists). Word order is noun phrase + modifiers typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s237-g155: چو introduces a temporal clause here and means 'when'. شیده is an epithet meaning 'mad, frenzied' (not 'love‑struck' in this context) and is the subject of the clause. بر + و (the preposition and conjunction) link the following noun: بر ... یال رستم reads as 'at / upon the mane and crest of Rostam' in compressed poetic order. بدید is the past-tense verb 'saw' (third person singular).
segments.s237-g156: یکی here is an indefinite numeral/adverb 'a certain' or simply 'a' (one). باد سرد is the noun phrase 'cold wind / cold blast' (باد = 'wind', سرد = 'cold'). از introduces the source phrase 'from', and جگر is used idiomatically as 'heart / seat of feeling' (metaphorical). برکشید is a poetic past tense form meaning 'rose up / surged' (literally 'lifted up'), so the whole clause says a chill rose from his heart.
segments.s237-g157: پدر را is a dative/indirect-object phrase 'to the father' (را marks the definite object/topic). چنین گفت is the reporting phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said' — note the supplied senses for گفت in the data do not include the simple past-verb sense, so treat it as the narrative past 'he said'). کین زورمند is an epithet/noun phrase introduced after the reporting verb, here functioning as the topic/accusative of what follows: 'that (kīn) powerful (zor-mand)' — 'کین' often carries sense of enmity/vengeance but here is part of a descriptive phrase 'that mighty (in anger/vengeance)'.
segments.s237-g158: که introduces a relative clause 'who/whom'. خوانی is the verb 'call/name' in an older/poetic form meaning 'you call' or 'you name' (the provided senses include 'to call/summon'); it functions here as the speech-act verb inside the relative clause. ورا is the clitic object 'to him'; the clause reads 'whom you call Rostam Divband' — رستم دیوبند is the name/epithet used as the complement of خوانی.
segments.s237-g159: بدین is an archaic/compressed prepositional phrase meaning 'by/with this' (here split in the word list as ب + دین); بدین برز بالا literally 'with this exalted might' — برز بالا is a compound meaning 'high/exalted might/force'. و این دست برد links the second instrument 'and with this hand' (این دست) to the verb برد (an archaic/poetic past form of بردن 'to take/bring/strike' — the supplied senses for برد do not include the verbal past sense, so treat it as the narrative verb 'he brought/struck'). The sequence lists two instruments of action: 'by this power and by this hand'.
segments.s237-g160: بخاک اندر آرد is a verb phrase 'brings (it) into the earth / to the ground' where بخاک = 'to the ground', اندر = 'into/in', and آرد is an archaic third-person singular present/subjunctive or narrative form of آوردن 'to bring' (here 'he brings/laid'); سر دیو is the object 'the demon's head' (سر = 'head', دیو = 'demon'). گرد at the end is a poetic particle/lexeme used in compounds (here completing the phrase) — the supplied senses for گرد ('around/about') do not fully capture its idiomatic, verbalizing/auxiliary use in classical lines, but functionally the whole clause says he brought the demon's head down to the ground.
